highlight.title: Critical React Server Components Vulnerability Creates React2Shell Exploits
highlight.summary: CVE-2025-55182 represents a 10.0 CVSS remote code execution vulnerability in React Server Components affecting Next.js 15-16. The vulnerability stems from insecure deserialization in the RSC Flight protocol, allowing attackers to achieve unauthenticated RCE through crafted HTTP requests.

resources.title: Next.js 16 Release: Cache Components with PPR & React Compiler Stable
resources.why_now: Next.js 16 introduces Cache Components as a new programming model leveraging Partial Pre-Rendering (PPR) and 'use cache' for instant navigation. The built-in React Compiler support is now stable, offering automatic memoization for production apps.

resources.title: TanStack Start vs Next.js: The Type-Safe Alternative Gaining Momentum
resources.why_now: TanStack Start is emerging as a serious Next.js competitor in 2025, offering lightweight runtime, end-to-end type safety, and Vite-powered development. It defaults to interactive components while allowing opt-in Server Components, addressing developer frustrations with Next.js complexity.

resources.title: React Server Components + TanStack Query: The 2026 Hybrid Architecture
resources.why_now: The hybrid RSC + TanStack Query pattern is becoming the standard for modern React apps in 2026. Teams report 40-70% faster initial loads by combining server-side rendering for static content with client-side caching for interactive features.

resources.title: Full-Stack JavaScript Development Patterns with TypeScript in 2026
resources.why_now: TypeScript has moved from preference to default for production JavaScript codebases. The guide covers proven patterns for shared types between frontend and backend, with 66% of developers now using JavaScript full-stack according to Stack Overflow 2025 survey.

claude_take: The React ecosystem reached an inflection point in 2025-2026, with serious security vulnerabilities in RSC highlighting the complexity costs of server-first architectures. Meanwhile, TanStack Start's rise as a type-safe, developer-centric alternative signals growing demand for explicit control over magical conventions. The real winners are teams adopting hybrid patterns that combine RSC for static content with proven client-side libraries like TanStack Query for interactivity.
